jokes.text: It's like, we're old enough to drink, we're old enough to chaperone a high school dance, to vote. Like that did fuck anything.
jokes.setup: Ilana is listing adult milestones to establish they should control their own heat.
jokes.punchline: Voting is casually dropped into the list of adult rights, then immediately undercut with '...like that did fuck anything' — a sideways Trump-era political joke.
jokes.explanation: Sharp mid-list pivot that recontextualizes the entire complaint. The casual contempt for voting feels very Ilana — she'd never be earnest about civic participation — and the economy of the dismissal lands. Not legendary but genuinely well-constructed.

jokes.text: And I was this close to being able to afford the Hot Dream Q20-X space heater. It's the one that Drew Barrymore uses.
jokes.setup: Ilana is explaining why she wants a specific space heater.
jokes.punchline: The brand name is ridiculous-sounding and the justification is that Drew Barrymore uses it — as if that's a credible consumer report.
jokes.explanation: The fake brand name is a nice absurdist touch and Drew Barrymore as a shopping authority is funny in its randomness. Feels like an Ilana-specific joke because she would absolutely track a celebrity's appliance choices.

jokes.text: Do you, like, get her credit card statements or something?
jokes.setup: Ilana cites Drew Barrymore as her endorsement for a space heater.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's dry follow-up implies Ilana's celebrity knowledge is so specific it must be surveilled.
jokes.explanation: Perfectly economical straight-man line. One sentence, maximum skepticism. Abbi's incredulity works because she's the audience surrogate here.

jokes.text: I think she's got great taste, and, like, a pixie spirit.
jokes.setup: Abbi questions how Ilana knows Drew Barrymore uses the heater.
jokes.punchline: Ilana doubles down with 'pixie spirit' as though this is a legitimate consumer trust signal.
jokes.explanation: 'Pixie spirit' as a product endorsement qualifier is funny and character-specific. Only Ilana would say this with absolute sincerity.

jokes.text: I'm a waitress. I am for real rich, not Trump rich.
jokes.setup: Ilana offers to buy Abbi the heater, claiming she can afford it.
jokes.punchline: She clarifies she's 'for real rich' — i.e., actually rich on her waitress salary — while specifying 'not Trump rich,' implying Trump's wealth is fake or fraudulent.
jokes.explanation: The inversion is sharp — a broke waitress claiming to be 'more real rich' than a billionaire is good absurdist logic, and the Trump joke earns it because the show is openly political this season. Economy is the standout quality.

jokes.text: I'm a little hard right now.
jokes.setup: Abbi says it's hard right now without a job.
jokes.punchline: Ilana mishears or deliberately co-opts 'hard' in a sexual context, confirming her constant state of arousal.
jokes.explanation: Classic Ilana wordplay, establishes her horniness as a character fixture for the episode. The pun itself isn't especially clever but the delivery context — mid-a real conversation about finances — creates good comic timing.

jokes.text: About this, Abigail. The most powerful moment in a woman's life: Your first gray hair!
jokes.setup: There's a scream from the bathroom — Abbi and the roommate rush over expecting something bad.
jokes.punchline: Ilana has been theatrically summoning them to announce a gray hair with the gravity of a life-or-death emergency.
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection — the panic of a scream leading to a gray hair revelation. Ilana framing it as 'the most powerful moment in a woman's life' is pure character comedy and sets up the witch theme beautifully.

jokes.text: I'm full-on flooding with envy.
jokes.setup: The roommate asks what's going on with Abbi's gray hair.
jokes.punchline: Ilana responds with 'flooding with envy' over a gray hair — and 'flooding' does double duty given her established sexuality.
jokes.explanation: 'Flooding' is doing subtle double work here — it's Ilana's vocabulary which always has a sexual undertone — while being genuinely expressive of outsized emotion. Good economy.

jokes.text: Don't you see? You're becoming a witch. A dope and powerful fucking witch. Like Dame Judi Dench. And Stacey London from 'What Not to Wear' before you. You're ascending.
jokes.setup: Abbi is upset about her gray hair as a sign of aging.
jokes.punchline: Ilana reframes gray hair as 'ascending' into witchhood — and her reference points are Dame Judi Dench AND Stacey London from 'What Not to Wear,' which is a hilariously mismatched pair of cultural icons.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's sharpest jokes. The Dame Judi Dench / Stacey London pairing is inspired — one is a prestige icon, one is a cable fashion host — and placing them in the same canon of 'gray-haired witches ascending' is absurd and funny. 'Ascending' delivered with total sincerity makes it land. Only Ilana would construct this mythology and believe in it completely. The joke also sets the entire thematic spine of the episode.

jokes.text: Witches aren't real, you guys.
jokes.setup: Ilana has just delivered an elaborate speech about Abbi ascending to witchhood.
jokes.punchline: The roommate's flat, unimpressed interjection — 'Witches aren't real, you guys' — is the perfect straight-man response.
jokes.explanation: Well-timed straight-man beat. The 'you guys' construction implies exhausted familiarity with Ilana's flights of fancy.

jokes.text: Dude, if witches aren't real, then who makes all the kombucha? And where do scarves come from? Scarf tree?
jokes.setup: The roommate has flatly stated witches aren't real.
jokes.punchline: Ilana presents kombucha and scarves as evidence of witchcraft — delivered with the logic of someone who genuinely believes this is airtight reasoning.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's funniest pure joke. The logic is airtight within its own absurd framework — kombucha and scarves ARE stereotypically associated with a certain crunchy, witchy woman archetype — and 'Scarf tree?' as a contemptuous dismissal of the alternative explanation is brilliant. It's quotable, original, perfectly Ilana, and structured beautifully. The sarcastic 'Scarf tree?' deserves special credit for its economy and contempt.

jokes.text: I just thought that by the time I'd have gray hair, I'd have...a MacArthur Genius Grant or at least be posting pictures of 'akai' bowls on my Instagram account.
jokes.setup: Abbi is lamenting that getting a gray hair at her age wasn't part of her life plan.
jokes.punchline: The bar goes from 'MacArthur Genius Grant' all the way down to 'posting acai bowls on Instagram' — a beautifully calibrated lowering of ambition that's also painfully relatable.
jokes.explanation: The descending ambition structure is excellent — genius grant to acai bowl Instagram is a perfectly calibrated fall. It's character-specific (Abbi is the aspirational one), generationally specific, and genuinely funny. The mispronunciation of 'acai' as 'akai' is a nice organic touch of character authenticity.

jokes.text: Acai. / Whatever.
jokes.setup: Abbi mispronounces 'acai' as 'akai.'
jokes.punchline: Ilana corrects her; Abbi dismisses the correction immediately — she doesn't care enough about acai bowls to learn to say them right.
jokes.explanation: The 'Whatever' is a nice micro-joke. Abbi's existential spiral can't even accommodate the correct pronunciation of a trendy food. Quick and clean.

jokes.text: Double, double, toil and trouble, fire burn and cauldron bubble, bitch!
jokes.setup: Abbi decides to take action and buy the space heater herself.
jokes.punchline: Ilana punctuates Abbi's empowerment moment with Shakespearean witch-verse, adding 'bitch' at the end.
jokes.explanation: The 'bitch' tag on Macbeth is a reliable Ilana move and it earns its laugh because the earnestness of the incantation makes the profanity capper feel both aggressive and affectionate simultaneously.

jokes.text: 250 camp friends? / But not anymore. I am selling 'em.
jokes.setup: Ilana announces she has 250 handmade Christmas cards she was going to send to 250 camp friends.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's reaction shot — '250 camp friends?' — does the work. Then Ilana pivots to selling them, dropping the camp friends entirely without ceremony.
jokes.explanation: '250 camp friends' is a throwaway detail that reveals Ilana's world is inexplicably vast and social. The immediate pivot to selling them without guilt is good character work.

jokes.text: I just have to meet up with my dad for pedicures. Oh, I thought that you said that you were doing the ice skating with your mom today. Whole... family day planned.
jokes.setup: Ilana agrees to help Abbi but mentions overlapping family commitments.
jokes.punchline: The accumulation of family activities — pedicures with dad, ice skating with mom, eventually 'annual family massage' — reveals Ilana has an absurdly packed and luxurious family day scheduled.
jokes.explanation: This is the seed of a running gag — Ilana's implausibly rich family social calendar. Rewatch bonus because you catch all the installments on subsequent viewings.

jokes.text: I just gotta make sure that I find the time for a powerful genital sneeze, or four. 'Cause I'm a cum kween!
jokes.setup: Ilana is describing her packed day.
jokes.punchline: 'Powerful genital sneeze' as a euphemism for orgasm — and then escalating it to 'or four.'
jokes.explanation: 'Genital sneeze' is a genuinely creative euphemism — involuntary, bodily, not romantic — that reframes orgasms as a physiological need rather than a sexual one. Combined with 'or four' it captures Ilana's relationship to her own sexuality with perfect economy. High originality score because this specific phrasing is novel.

jokes.text: I just have to come sometimes. Like today, so fucking much, so maybe you guys can get the fuck out so I can jerk off real quick.
jokes.setup: Ilana has established she needs to orgasm before her day.
jokes.punchline: She asks Abbi and the roommate to leave her apartment so she can masturbate, casually, mid-conversation.
jokes.explanation: The casual, unapologetic request lands because Ilana treats it as completely reasonable. The specificity of 'real quick' implies efficiency over intimacy, which is funnier than if she were precious about it.

jokes.text: What? / What? / I'm okay.
jokes.setup: Ilana has just asked her friends to leave so she can masturbate.
jokes.punchline: The double-beat of Abbi and the roommate saying 'What?' simultaneously, followed by Ilana's self-reassuring 'I'm okay' — implying she's aware she just said something odd but stands by it.
jokes.explanation: 'I'm okay' as self-reassurance after saying something outrageous is a comedy beat Ilana does well. The double 'What?' is standard but functional. The entire sequence works as an escalating three-part micro-scene.

jokes.text: Am I allowed to behave that way?
jokes.setup: After asking her friends to leave so she can masturbate, Ilana addresses the camera in a quasi-confessional beat.
jokes.punchline: She sincerely wonders whether her behavior was socially appropriate — which implies she has genuine doubt, but also implies she'd do it again regardless.
jokes.explanation: This is a beautifully self-aware beat. The question is meta — she's asking the audience — but it's also totally in character because Ilana occasionally has flickers of social self-awareness that immediately fade. The fact that she has to ask suggests she already knows the answer is probably no, but she'll proceed anyway.

jokes.text: Knish, k'witch?
jokes.setup: Abbi is setting up her table outside the Met with Margo.
jokes.punchline: A street vendor offers a portmanteau of 'knish' and 'which' — or possibly 'witch' — as a pun sales pitch.
jokes.explanation: A quick, light pun that establishes the street vendor's energy. Not especially original but playful and New York-specific.

jokes.text: Well, with the BB&B coupon, it comes to, like, 69 bucks... / Oh, horny number! Ooh, yowza, love it!
jokes.setup: Abbi tells Margo how much she needs to sell to afford the space heater.
jokes.punchline: Margo reacts to the number 69 with outsized sexual enthusiasm — 'Oh, horny number!' — which both reveals her character and connects to the episode's sexuality theme.
jokes.explanation: 69 jokes are low-hanging fruit but 'horny number' is a funnier way to call it out than most, and Margo's 'yowza' gives her instant character flavor. Functional but not distinguished.

jokes.text: It's kinda like the Wexlers Take Manhattan. We actually call it that. / It's cute. / Yeah. Totally.
jokes.setup: Ilana describes her family's day in Manhattan.
jokes.punchline: The Wexler family has officially branded their own family outings after a Woody Allen film, and say it with total unselfconscious pride.
jokes.explanation: The Wexlers naming their own family outing is a great character detail — they're Jewish, New York, intellectually pretentious, unironic. Abbi's 'It's cute' / 'Yeah. Totally' reveals she has no idea how to respond to this information.

jokes.text: Would you like a hit of some soup? / Wow. Homemade lentil.
jokes.setup: Margo, an older woman on the Met steps, approaches Abbi.
jokes.punchline: She offers 'a hit of some soup' — framing soup like a drug — with a thermos, establishing her as a fully formed eccentric.
jokes.explanation: 'A hit of some soup' is a lovely turn of phrase that immediately characterizes Margo as witchy, communal, and slightly off. The visual of a thermos being offered like a joint is strong.

jokes.text: Oh, my goddess. You two have the same thermos. / Same wheelie cart, too. / Soul sisters.
jokes.setup: Abbi and Margo both have the same thermos.
jokes.punchline: Ilana elevates matching thermoses and wheelie carts to a spiritual sign of 'soul sisters,' treating mundane coincidence as cosmic destiny.
jokes.explanation: Good escalation — thermos to wheelie cart to 'soul sisters' — with the supernatural interpretation of a totally mundane coincidence. Serves the witch theme efficiently.

jokes.text: It's actually the only one where the wheels don't turn to squares when you roll over snow salt. / That was spooky.
jokes.setup: Abbi and Margo both have the same wheelie cart and begin explaining it simultaneously.
jokes.punchline: They finish each other's sentence about an extremely specific product feature — and Ilana reacts 'That was spooky' as if it's paranormal rather than a shopping coincidence.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'wheels don't turn to squares when you roll over snow salt' is a very real and annoying wheelie cart problem, which makes the synchronicity funnier. 'That was spooky' is the perfect Ilana reaction — treating consumer product enthusiasm as supernatural.

jokes.text: That Margo's a mighty witch. She nearly gave me a clit, throat and butt-gasm.
jokes.setup: Margo has walked away after their interaction.
jokes.punchline: Ilana claims Margo's presence nearly triggered a multi-location orgasm — casually listing anatomical zones.
jokes.explanation: The list format — clit, throat, butt — delivered clinically is funnier than any individual item. Also works as foreshadowing: Ilana's sexuality is building toward the episode's climax (literally). Very Ilana in its frankness and specificity.

jokes.text: I'm a crazy, sick sex addict, like David Duchovny in that show, and in real life.
jokes.setup: Abbi calls out Ilana for being weird about Margo.
jokes.punchline: Ilana self-diagnoses as a sex addict and cites David Duchovny's fictional role AND his real-life tabloid scandal as equivalent reference points.
jokes.explanation: 'In that show, and in real life' is a smart comedic addition — Duchovny's Californication sex addict character and his real-world sex addiction are merged into one. The show earns the joke by having done the research.

jokes.text: Better jerk off before I get on the subway. / Ew, Ilana, chill. / Later, Ab. Super wet!
jokes.setup: Ilana is leaving for her family day.
jokes.punchline: She announces her subway masturbation plan casually, and signs off with 'Super wet!' as a farewell.
jokes.explanation: 'Super wet!' as a farewell is a funny callback to Ilana's constant sexualized state and works as a running character gag. The subway masturbation plan is cringe-funny in its audacity.

jokes.text: So, Ilana... Show me your vulva.
jokes.setup: Ilana has arrived at what appears to be a normal apartment visit. Betty has been making small talk.
jokes.punchline: Betty's immediate clinical pivot to 'Show me your vulva' reveals this is a sex therapist / gynecologist consultation — and the abruptness of it is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Maximum economy — four words — for a massive tonal shift. The setup builds through pleasant apartment small talk and then 'Show me your vulva' lands like a dropped anvil. The directness is both clinical and shocking. The scene cut after makes it even better, implying Ilana complied immediately.

jokes.text: So basically, I've had more sex than, like, anybody in the entire world. Not just average sex, but, like, dank '70s porno sex. I can squirt, and like, spray. I leave a cum trail wherever I go, kind of like a slutty slug.
jokes.setup: Ilana is presenting her sexual credentials to Betty as context for why she's there.
jokes.punchline: The escalation from 'lots of sex' to 'dank '70s porno sex' to 'cum trail like a slutty slug' — each step is more graphic and more specific.
jokes.explanation: 'Slutty slug' is a memorably disgusting and very funny image — the alliteration, the gastropod comparison — and it's peak Ilana. The escalation structure is well-executed. 'Dank '70s porno sex' has a specific flavor that makes it funnier than just saying 'great sex.'

jokes.text: It is my friend Abbi's problem, so I'm just here for her by proxy. She hasn't, uh, orgasmed in months. And she's too embarrassed to come, L-O-L, here, to see you.
jokes.setup: After presenting her own extraordinary sexual credentials, Ilana pivots to explain why she's really there.
jokes.punchline: She's there for Abbi 'by proxy' — while being examined herself — and then spells out 'L-O-L' after making a come/orgasm pun, which is simultaneously meta and dumb.
jokes.explanation: Spelling out 'L-O-L' is a very Ilana-specific comedic choice — she would absolutely say this aloud. The 'come here' pun is gentle wordplay but the self-aware annotation is what makes it Ilana-specific.

jokes.text: So your friend hasn't come in months and you're here for her. / Exactly.
jokes.setup: Ilana has explained that she's attending the sex therapy session on behalf of Abbi.
jokes.punchline: Betty's gentle summary — delivered with maximum neutrality — and Ilana's bright 'Exactly!' implies she sees nothing absurd about this arrangement.
jokes.explanation: Betty's deadpan processing of the situation is funny in its clinical acceptance. The joke is that Betty just rolls with it, which makes the dynamic feel lived-in and real.

jokes.text: Okay. If that's what you want to do. [beat]
jokes.setup: Betty has accepted that Ilana is there as a proxy for Abbi.
jokes.punchline: The pause after Betty's professional acceptance implies she's treating the situation with total therapeutic legitimacy — which is funnier than if she pushed back.
jokes.explanation: The comedy here is in the silence — Betty just professionally accepts a completely bizarre arrangement. Short but effective as a scene-setter.

jokes.text: Would you like a puff of my wand? It's pretty magical.
jokes.setup: Margo is continuing to establish her witchy persona for Abbi.
jokes.punchline: 'Puff of my wand' — a weed/witch pun — offered with serene generosity.
jokes.explanation: 'My wand' as a weed pipe is a neat pun that keeps the witch metaphor consistent. 'It's pretty magical' as a weed endorsement is a good understatement.

jokes.text: You know, most people don't realize this, but you lose most of your body heat from your upper wing area.
jokes.setup: Margo notices Abbi is cold.
jokes.punchline: 'Upper wing area' — she refers to shoulders as wings, continuing the witch mythology with total conviction.
jokes.explanation: Margo's consistent reframing of body parts in mystical terms is a great character tic. 'Wing area' vs. shoulders is a small but perfectly executed world-building joke.

jokes.text: Hey! Same Tupperware! / Oh, yeah. / Uh, twinsies, I guess.
jokes.setup: Margo and Abbi both pull out the same Tupperware for lunch.
jokes.punchline: A third matching-objects coincidence in a row — the universe keeps insisting they are cosmically connected through consumer products.
jokes.explanation: The third-time escalation of the matching consumer goods joke lands well. The rule of three is being deployed correctly here — thermos, wheelie cart, Tupperware — and Abbi's resigned 'twinsies, I guess' shows she's starting to believe despite herself.

jokes.text: Beautiful, isn't she? / Straight up gorgeous.
jokes.setup: Betty is showing Ilana something in a mirror or diagram during the therapy session.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's 'straight up gorgeous' reaction to her own vulva — presumably what Betty is showing her — is delivered with total pride.
jokes.explanation: Ilana's relationship to her own body is a consistent character trait — she finds herself beautiful without narcissism, just honest self-appreciation. 'Straight up gorgeous' as a vulva assessment is funny in its genuine pride.

jokes.text: Ilana, I want you to name your vulva. / What about... 'Abbi'?
jokes.setup: Betty asks Ilana to name her vulva as a therapeutic exercise.
jokes.punchline: Ilana names it after her best friend — without hesitation — which is simultaneously the most and least surprising answer.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best joke. The character integration score is near-perfect because ONLY Ilana would name her vulva after Abbi — it's a perfect expression of their friendship's intimacy, her lack of boundaries, and her genuine love for Abbi. The payoff is funny but also weirdly touching, which is the Broad City sweet spot. It sets up the entire therapy monologue that follows and retroactively makes the subsequent insults funnier. Maximum economy — four words after the setup.

jokes.text: Abbi... you've been stubborn lately. Like, withholding. You're so full of shit. You can't even focus enough to relax. You're not even a vulva or a vagina. You're just a butthole that happens to bleed. I fucking hate you, bitch! Fuck you!
jokes.setup: Betty asks Ilana to have a conversation with 'Abbi' (her vulva) to express her feelings.
jokes.punchline: Ilana begins politely and escalates rapidly to 'you're just a butthole that happens to bleed' and 'I fucking hate you, bitch' — full rage at her own genitalia.
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'stubborn' to 'butthole that happens to bleed' is perfectly constructed — it goes through recognizable relationship fight language before landing on a spectacular anatomical insult that's also somehow medically accurate. The callback structure (this is Ilana talking to 'Abbi' the vulva, not Abbi the person) adds a layer of comedy because the insults could apply to both. 'Butthole that happens to bleed' is a genuinely creative piece of writing.

jokes.text: It's okay. It's just the first layer: rage.
jokes.setup: Ilana has just screamed at her own vulva in a therapy session.
jokes.punchline: Betty's clinical calm — 'It's just the first layer: rage' — treats this like a completely normal therapeutic progression.
jokes.explanation: Betty's therapeutic composure in the face of Ilana's chaos is a great straight-man beat. 'The first layer: rage' implies there are more layers, which creates dread-anticipation that pays off beautifully.

jokes.text: I know it's not you. It's me. I increased my dosage of antidepressants. And I've also just been more anxious and depressed, this whole disgusting, gross year. And now I have dead pussy.
jokes.setup: Betty asks Ilana to go deeper and tell 'Abbi' what's really in her heart.
jokes.punchline: Ilana shifts from rage to genuine vulnerability — antidepressants, anxiety, depression — before landing on 'dead pussy' as the clinical summary.
jokes.explanation: This is genuinely brave comedy writing. Ilana addresses SSRI-related sexual dysfunction — a real and underrepresented issue — with the exact right mixture of comedy and honesty. 'Dead pussy' as the summary term is funny AND painful, which is the best kind of dark comedy. The show earns this by having Ilana be genuinely vulnerable rather than just outrageous.

jokes.text: Dead pussy. / [pause] / Dead pussy. / [longer pause] / It's essentially dead.
jokes.setup: Ilana has just confessed to having 'dead pussy.'
jokes.punchline: The phrase is repeated three times by both characters with increasing gravity — each repetition sinks it further into uncomfortable territory before 'it's essentially dead' delivers the final beat.
jokes.explanation: The comedic power of repetition with escalating silence is expertly deployed here. Each 'dead pussy' lands harder than the last, and 'essentially dead' as a qualifying retreat is a perfect closer. This is a structural joke — the writing knows that the repetition IS the joke.

jokes.text: How about a little hug for the Donald? / It's like a magnet. / Grab 'em by the pussy. / USA! USA! USA! / Grab 'em... Grab 'em by the pussy. / I won. I won.
jokes.setup: Betty is attempting some therapeutic hands-on work. Ilana's brain is pulled into the Trump-as-sexual-intrusion metaphor.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's internal visualization of Trump's 'grab 'em by the pussy' boast — with Trump's voice/image apparently intruding on her arousal — explains the orgasm blockage. The 'USA! USA!' chant and 'I won. I won.' complete the nightmare image.
jokes.explanation: Using Trump's most infamous quote as a literal orgasm-blocker is inspired — the joke is political but also genuinely insightful about how trauma intrudes on intimacy. 'I won. I won.' as Trump's victorious post-intrusion declaration is horrifying in exactly the right way. This is dark comedy with genuine bite.

jokes.text: Game show president *** in chief! / I haven't come since the election! And come is a lot of who I am. I'm a cum kween.
jokes.setup: Ilana has experienced the Trump mental intrusion during her therapy session.
jokes.punchline: 'Game show president commander in chief' as a Trump descriptor, followed by 'come is a lot of who I am' — connecting political trauma to personal identity in a way that's only possible for Ilana.
jokes.explanation: 'Come is a lot of who I am' is an unexpectedly moving line in context — Ilana's sexuality is genuinely central to her identity, and Trump has taken that from her. The 'cum kween' callback resonates differently here because it's now about loss. 'Game show president' is a good coinage.

jokes.text: Orgasms have been down 140% since Trump was elected. / Ugh! It's been horrible for everyone. / Great for me, though. I'm technically the only small business owner that that human skin tag has ever helped.
jokes.setup: Betty validates Ilana's Trump-related sexual dysfunction with a statistic.
jokes.punchline: Betty cheerfully notes that Trump has been great for her business (sex therapy), calling him a 'human skin tag' — a precise and disgusting descriptor.
jokes.explanation: 'Human skin tag' is an exceptional piece of political insult writing — it's medical, visual, perfectly contemptuous, and somehow not overused. Betty's acknowledgment that she's the only small business Trump has helped is a sharp economic/political joke. '140%' is a funny impossible statistic. This entire beat fires on multiple levels.

jokes.text: Great for me, though. I'm technically the only small business owner that that human skin tag has ever helped.
jokes.setup: Betty says orgasms being down 140% has been horrible for everyone
jokes.punchline: She pivots to self-interest — her sex therapy business is booming because of Trump — and 'human skin tag' is an excellent, precise insult
jokes.explanation: 'Human skin tag' as a Trump descriptor is genuinely excellent — it's specific, slightly medical, visually evocative (useless, fleshy, dangly), and avoids the more obvious insults. 'Technically the only small business owner that human skin tag has ever helped' is a well-constructed sentence that lands both as political comedy and character work for Betty.

jokes.text: Oh. Oh, um, you have a little... / It's a gray! / Uh, no. On your nose. / Oh, it's a lentil. It's a lentil from way... earlier. I don't know how that got up there.
jokes.setup: The dermatologist is examining Abbi and notices something.
jokes.punchline: Abbi leaps to 'It's a gray!' — worried about aging — but it's just a lentil on her nose from Margo's soup. The embarrassed 'I don't know how that got up there' is the cherry.
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the lentil soup, and good misdirection — Abbi's paranoia about aging makes her see gray hairs everywhere. 'I don't know how that got up there' is the right note of embarrassment. The dermatologist's flat delivery heightens the awkwardness.

jokes.text: An Upper East Side dermatologist, if you catch my drift. / Yeah, okay, I see. / But what do you mean by that?
jokes.setup: The dermatologist implies her specialty without spelling it out.
jokes.punchline: Abbi says 'Yeah, okay, I see' — implying she understands — and then immediately asks 'But what do you mean by that?' — revealing she has no idea.
jokes.explanation: The micro-beat of Abbi bluffing comprehension and then immediately revealing she doesn't understand is a classic but well-executed character moment. It's Abbi's specific social awkwardness — she'd rather claim to understand than admit confusion.

jokes.text: Let's just say I can get my hands on whatever a girl needs to stay fresh and young-looking. Botox, fillers, bat feces.
jokes.setup: The dermatologist is explaining her black-market-adjacent cosmetic offerings.
jokes.punchline: The list goes Botox, fillers — normal — then 'bat feces' — not normal — delivered with identical clinical confidence.
jokes.explanation: Classic comedy list structure where the third item is the absurd escalation. 'Bat feces' is a great third-item choice — disgusting, vaguely plausible as a real beauty treatment, and delivered with the same confidence as Botox. Good economy.

jokes.text: I'm still in my 20s. I mean, the gray is super premature, and the hunchback is just a scarf to protect my wings.
jokes.setup: The dermatologist has given Abbi her business card and suggested she 'freshen up.'
jokes.punchline: Abbi's defensive internal monologue — to the camera or herself — reveals she's absorbed Margo's 'wings' terminology while also being oddly insecure about something she just called a 'hunchback.'
jokes.explanation: Good callback to Margo's wing metaphor, but what makes this work is 'the hunchback is just a scarf to protect my wings' — Abbi has unconsciously admitted she might have a hunchback while defending it. Self-deprecating and character-specific.

jokes.text: I went to MICA, too! / You could hear all that? / Spooky.
jokes.setup: After the dermatologist leaves, Margo reveals she heard the conversation.
jokes.punchline: Margo also went to MICA — another impossible coincidence — and Abbi's 'You could hear all that?' paired with Margo's smug 'Spooky' reinforces her as supernaturally attuned.
jokes.explanation: The MICA coincidence completes the running 'soul sisters' gag and Margo's 'Spooky' is the right self-satisfied button. The recurring structure of Abbi doubting the supernatural and getting more evidence is paying off nicely here.

jokes.text: You're traumatized by the fact that a sexual-assault bragging steak salesman has become our president.
jokes.setup: Betty is diagnosing why Ilana can't orgasm.
jokes.punchline: 'Sexual-assault bragging steak salesman' as a three-word descriptor of Trump — combining his Access Hollywood tape, Trump Steaks, and his salesman persona.
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional descriptive writing. 'Sexual-assault bragging steak salesman' packs three specific and accurate Trump attributes into six words with perfect economy. It's a coinage that could genuinely enter the cultural lexicon. The choice to include 'steak salesman' — referencing the failed Trump Steaks enterprise — is inspired and proves the writers went beyond the obvious.

jokes.text: Ew! Wait! Ugh! / God, I'm never gonna come again! / It's not going to be easy. Stay in the moment. / Fuck you, Trump! / I'm going back in.
jokes.setup: Ilana is attempting to orgasm in therapy with Trump's image intruding.
jokes.punchline: The sequence of disgust, defeat, encouragement, profanity, and determination — culminating in 'I'm going back in' — plays like an athletic training montage for masturbation.
jokes.explanation: 'I'm going back in' as a battle cry for masturbation is a funny and specific Ilana phrasing. The athletic/military framing of orgasm pursuit is consistent with the show's approach to female sexuality as an achievement and right.

jokes.text: Oh, my God. Abbi? / Jeremy?
jokes.setup: Abbi is at her table when she runs into an old neighbor she clearly had a crush on.
jokes.punchline: The uncomfortable ex-adjacent encounter — which the subsequent dialogue makes much worse.
jokes.explanation: Setup beat for the upcoming cringe sequence. Not funny in itself but structurally necessary. The real jokes come from what follows.

jokes.text: Natalie, this is Abbi, my old neighbor. / 'Old'?
jokes.setup: Jeremy introduces Abbi to his partner.
jokes.punchline: Abbi reacts to 'old neighbor' — the word 'old' — which reveals her insecurity about aging and about being defined by a past relationship.
jokes.explanation: One word — 'Old'? — does a lot of work. It connects to the episode's aging anxiety, to Abbi's complicated feelings about Jeremy, and to her current life circumstances. Very economical character beat.

jokes.text: Hi, Zabby! / [beat]
jokes.setup: Natalie greets Abbi.
jokes.punchline: 'Zabby' — a mangled version of Abbi — delivered cheerfully, implying Jeremy hasn't talked about Abbi enough for his partner to even get her name right.
jokes.explanation: 'Zabby' is a small perfect cringe moment — Natalie's enthusiasm in saying the wrong name is funnier than if she'd been hostile. The wrong name implies Abbi doesn't matter enough for precision, which stings more.

jokes.text: Oh, no, no, no. No, no, we're not married. She's not my property.
jokes.setup: Abbi congratulates Jeremy on being married.
jokes.punchline: Jeremy's progressive overcorrection — 'she's not my property' — is delivered with the earnestness of someone who takes feminism very seriously in a slightly annoying way.
jokes.explanation: A small but sharp character study of progressive male overcorrection — the 'she's not my property' is a real type of thing real people say, and it's mildly cringe-inducing in the right way.

jokes.text: We're best friends. Soul mates. Co-parents.
jokes.setup: Jeremy is explaining their relationship status.
jokes.punchline: The escalating relationship descriptors — best friends, soul mates, co-parents — describe an increasingly intimate relationship while denying they're together romantically. 'Co-parents' of a dog (Milo) is the twist.
jokes.explanation: The list escalation works and 'co-parents' of a dog — revealed shortly after — retroactively makes the entire description funnier. The show understands that people who call their dog their child are funny.

jokes.text: Um, yeah, obviously, you're not married. This is not what I do for a living. This is... I lost a bet, so that's why.
jokes.setup: Abbi has just been through an awkward exchange and is now standing at her holiday card table.
jokes.punchline: She feels the need to explain that selling holiday cards is not her career, blaming it on a lost bet — a completely unnecessary lie that makes everything worse.
jokes.explanation: The instinct to lie about selling art cards when an ex appears is a very specific and recognizable social anxiety. 'I lost a bet' is the perfect cover story — plausible but also sad. This is character-specific cringe comedy that only Abbi would do.

jokes.text: Babe, we should get some. 'Thank you' cards, for the Planned Parenthood fundraiser.
jokes.setup: Jeremy's partner says they should buy Abbi's cards.
jokes.punchline: The cards Abbi made — Christmas cards — are being repurposed for a Planned Parenthood fundraiser, which is slightly but perfectly ironic given the episode's themes.
jokes.explanation: The Planned Parenthood detail is funny in context — the holiday cards being appropriated for a feminist cause — and connects to the episode's political spine. Not a stand-alone joke but good contextual comedy.

jokes.text: And I love helping struggling artists. / Actually, you know what? Keep it.
jokes.setup: Natalie offers to buy all the cards for $80.
jokes.punchline: Natalie's description of Abbi as a 'struggling artist' causes Abbi to try to give the money back — her pride reacts before her need for money can.
jokes.explanation: 'Keep it' as a reaction to 'struggling artist' is very Abbi — she'd rather lose money than accept charity from someone who pities her, especially in front of her ex. The instinct is recognizable and character-true.

jokes.text: I should be pounding that ass tonight.
jokes.setup: Jeremy's family has just left after a very awkward exchange.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's internal (or barely external) comment about Jeremy reveals she's still attracted to him despite everything. The timing — immediately after they leave — is perfect.
jokes.explanation: The bluntness of this line vs. the polite awkwardness of the prior interaction is a good tonal contrast. It's also funny that Abbi's frustration with the encounter doesn't kill her attraction — it intensifies it.

jokes.text: Money is a mind control technique that quantifies the progress of the patriarchy!
jokes.setup: Abbi asks Margo to watch her stuff while she goes to spend her earnings.
jokes.punchline: Margo's response to 'I got money to spend' is an anti-capitalist feminist manifesto about money being a patriarchal construct — followed immediately by 'Yeah, I'll watch your stuff.'
jokes.explanation: The structure is excellent — manifesto followed by immediate practical compliance. Margo's contradiction (she believes money is mind control but will still watch the table) is very human and funny. The speech itself is good absurdist feminist parody.

jokes.text: An average-sized dick... / Yes. / Electoral college! / Come on. / Fat titties. / Mike Pence! / Focus, focus...
jokes.setup: Ilana is attempting to achieve orgasm by thinking of arousing things, but Trump-related thoughts keep intruding.
jokes.punchline: The montage of erotic thoughts being interrupted by political horror — dick → electoral college, titties → Mike Pence — is a perfect joke structure that's also a genuine metaphor for how the election ruined pleasure.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's peak joke and one of the best political comedy bits of the Trump era. The structure is perfect: erotic image → political horror image, each pair calibrated (dick/electoral college, titties/Mike Pence). The joke works on multiple levels: it's funny as absurdism, it's accurate as political satire (Mike Pence specifically as the anti-titties figure), and it's a genuine representation of how political trauma invades personal space. 'Focus, focus...' as Ilana's coaching of herself is the perfect button. Only this show, only this character, only this moment in history.

jokes.text: Hairy taints... / Tiny, tiny hands! / Push through. Tap in. You are powerful. You are woman. / Yes. You... are... cum kween!
jokes.setup: The orgasm-attempt montage continues.
jokes.punchline: 'Hairy taints' → 'Tiny, tiny hands!' — the Trump hands joke used as an arousal-killer — followed by Betty's coaching, climaxing in the 'cum kween' declaration.
jokes.explanation: The 'tiny, tiny hands' as a Trump orgasm-killer is inspired because it uses Trump's own well-documented insecurity. 'Hairy taints' → 'Tiny, tiny hands!' is a perfect comic pairing. Betty's speech escalating to 'cum kween' takes the epithet from Ilana's self-applied label and makes it a genuine battle cry. The sequence is sustained excellence.

jokes.text: When they go low, we go high. Women can get stuff done.
jokes.setup: Ilana is in the final push toward orgasm.
jokes.punchline: Michelle Obama's 'when they go low, we go high' — a solemn political motto — is repurposed as Ilana's masturbation affirmation, recontextualizing both the quote and the act.
jokes.explanation: Using a Michelle Obama quote as orgasm motivation is a genuinely inspired joke — it's funny, political, and actually makes thematic sense in the episode's frame of reclaiming pleasure as feminist resistance. The recontextualization is elegant and earns the laugh without cheapening either element.

jokes.text: He can never, ever take away our cumming.
jokes.setup: Ilana is in the final moment of achieving orgasm.
jokes.punchline: The declaration of orgasmic independence — Trump cannot take this — delivered as a political statement of resistance.
jokes.explanation: This line is simultaneously funny and genuinely moving — it's the thesis of the episode. The use of 'our' makes it communal rather than individual. Only Ilana could make this declaration and have it be both absurd and sincere.

jokes.text: Looking good. Just let that sink in for a little longer before we hit the other side.
jokes.setup: Abbi is in the dermatologist's office receiving Botox on one side of her face.
jokes.punchline: The reveal that only one side of Abbi's face has been done — and it apparently looks noticeably different — sets up Abbi's growing regret.
jokes.explanation: Setup beat for Abbi's Botox regret arc. The joke is in the visual — one Botoxed side vs. one normal side — which we infer from context. Functional but the real laugh is the visual, which the transcript can only suggest.

jokes.text: That and 20 of its cousins. It's hard to be beautiful these days. It is my full-time job, but for most other women, it's their second full-time job where you're losing money.
jokes.setup: Abbi asks if the treatment is what the dermatologist uses personally.
jokes.punchline: The '20 of its cousins' implies a nightmarish quantity of beauty procedures, followed by the observation that beauty maintenance is women's second full-time job where they lose money — a sharp feminist critique delivered casually.
jokes.explanation: 'Second full-time job where you're losing money' is a sharp and quotable observation about beauty economics. The dermatologist saying it — the person who profits from this system — makes it mordantly ironic. Good dark comedy with feminist teeth.

jokes.text: Obviously, your chin is of concern.
jokes.setup: The dermatologist is about to upsell Abbi on more procedures.
jokes.punchline: 'Obviously' — the word implies Abbi should already know her chin is a problem — is a tiny but devastating word choice.
jokes.explanation: 'Obviously' is doing all the work — it implies a whole world of beauty anxiety that Abbi supposedly should already have. The follow-up about a 'Reese Witherspoon chin' is also funny as Abbi's defensive self-image.

jokes.text: I thought I...had a...like, a Reese Witherspoon chin.
jokes.setup: The dermatologist implies Abbi's chin is problematic.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's self-image of her chin as 'Reese Witherspoon' — a distinctive and famous chin — is a specific and funny benchmark to have chosen.
jokes.explanation: Reese Witherspoon's chin is specifically known — it's the distinctive point of her face — so Abbi using it as a self-flattery benchmark is both funny and weirdly specific. It reveals how Abbi has constructed her self-image.

jokes.text: I thought I... I had a... like, a Reese Witherspoon chin.
jokes.setup: Betty flags Abbi's chin as a concern
jokes.punchline: Abbi defends her chin by citing Reese Witherspoon — who is famous for having a notably prominent/angular chin, which is an iconic but unusual celebrity chin to aspire to. The comparison is both defensive and slightly absurd
jokes.explanation: Reese Witherspoon is a specific enough reference — her chin is genuinely famous/discussed — that the comparison lands. The joke is that Abbi has been silently proud of a chin feature that many would not consider aspirational. Character-specific humor.

jokes.text: Or, I can make you whiter.
jokes.setup: The dermatologist is upselling Abbi on cosmetic procedures.
jokes.punchline: The casual offer to 'make you whiter' — skin lightening as a casual upsell — is deeply uncomfortable as both a real practice and a satirical observation.
jokes.explanation: The bluntness of the offer is what makes it funny and uncomfortable simultaneously. The show doesn't editorialize — Abbi's 'Uh...' says everything. Dark comedy with real satirical intent about the beauty industry.

jokes.text: I think I'm really hot. / Usually. I usually feel super hot.
jokes.setup: Abbi is explaining why she's going to stop the Botox.
jokes.punchline: The qualifier 'Usually. I usually feel super hot.' immediately deflates the confident declaration — she's still processing the gray hair and the Jeremy encounter.
jokes.explanation: The self-correction is very Abbi — she wants to be confident but can't quite commit. 'Usually' doing all the work of undermining the preceding declaration is good character writing.

jokes.text: I think you're really beautiful. And I think that you would be even if you didn't do any of this crazy shit to your face.
jokes.setup: Abbi is complimenting the dermatologist.
jokes.punchline: Abbi gives a genuine compliment but tacks on 'crazy shit to your face' — a condemnation of the exact procedures Abbi just had done — without realizing the self-indictment.
jokes.explanation: The inadvertent self-burn — Abbi judging the dermatologist's cosmetic procedures while her own Botox is still settling — is a good character comedy beat. The sincerity of the compliment makes the 'crazy shit' more effective.

jokes.text: Oh, no. I laughed. [beat]
jokes.setup: The dermatologist has received Abbi's heartfelt compliment.
jokes.punchline: She laughs — and immediately realizes her Botoxed face has done something wrong. The pause after implies her face is doing something alarming.
jokes.explanation: Four words and a beat — 'Oh, no. I laughed.' — conveys the entire horror of a Botoxed face attempting an expression it can't properly make. The visual comedy inferred from context is likely spectacular (her face contorting oddly). Maximum economy for the setup; the visual does the rest. One of the tightest constructions in the episode.

jokes.text: Hey, drink up. You lost a lot of fluids during that humongous orgasm.
jokes.setup: Ilana has successfully orgasmed in therapy.
jokes.punchline: Betty's clinical aftercare advice — hydrate — treating a therapeutic orgasm like athletic recovery, with 'humongous' as the understated descriptor.
jokes.explanation: Betty's therapeutic professionalism applied to orgasm aftercare is consistently funny throughout the episode. 'Humongous' as an adjective for a therapeutic orgasm is good deadpan.

jokes.text: It's mostly weed, a little ginger and some cumin. / It's really good. / I think I'm gonna cüm again.
jokes.setup: Betty explains the aftercare drink contains cumin.
jokes.punchline: 'Cumin' → 'cüm again' — Ilana hears the spice name as a sexual verb, and the umlaut in the script implies she's stylizing it.
jokes.explanation: A well-executed spice/sex pun. The umlaut is a nice written comedy touch implying Ilana is orally stylizing the word even as she says it.

jokes.text: Yes! The floodgates are open now. You've got a lot of orgasming to do after a year of Trump-related pussy constipation.
jokes.setup: Ilana is experiencing multiple consecutive orgasms.
jokes.punchline: 'Trump-related pussy constipation' is a spectacularly accurate and funny coinage for the phenomenon the episode has been dramatizing.
jokes.explanation: 'Trump-related pussy constipation' deserves special commendation as a coinage — it's precise, funny, anatomically metaphorical, and politically specific. The word 'constipation' is exactly right because it implies buildup, blockage, and eventual relief. One of the most quotable lines in the episode.

jokes.text: I hate to 'gaz and dash, but I want to go tell Abbi what 'Abbi' accomplished today.
jokes.setup: Ilana is leaving Betty's apartment after her therapeutic orgasm.
jokes.punchline: 'Gaz and dash' — orgasm and dash — as a play on 'hit and run' or 'eat and dash.' Then the callback to the vulva named 'Abbi' and what it accomplished.
jokes.explanation: 'Gaz and dash' is an excellent coinage — economical, character-specific, and immediately comprehensible. The second part of the joke — telling Abbi what 'Abbi' accomplished — is a beautiful callback that works on both a friendship level and a comedy level.

jokes.text: I can't thank you enough for helping m... / Oh... / Ooh, it's happening again! Oh! It's gonna be a big one! Oh! / I'm gonna have to get these rugs cleaned.
jokes.setup: Ilana is trying to express gratitude and leave.
jokes.punchline: She orgasms mid-sentence while saying goodbye — and Betty's response is the pragmatic 'I'm gonna have to get these rugs cleaned.'
jokes.explanation: Betty's 'I'm gonna have to get these rugs cleaned' is the perfect straight-man exit line — practical, resigned, completely unfazed. The contrast between Ilana's ecstasy and Betty's housekeeping concern is excellent.

jokes.text: Oh! Ooh, it's happening again! Oh! It's gonna be a big one! Oh! I'm gonna have to get these rugs cleaned.
jokes.setup: Ilana is saying goodbye to Betty when another orgasm begins
jokes.punchline: Betty's calm 'I'm gonna have to get these rugs cleaned' — the professional's pragmatic response to an extremely disruptive event, stated while Ilana is mid-orgasm
jokes.explanation: Betty's rug comment is the punchline — it's the professional's appropriate response to Ilana's squirting, stated with the resignation of someone who deals with this regularly. 'These rugs' implies this has happened before. The escalation of Ilana's orgasm during goodbye and Betty's pragmatic response is excellent character comedy for both.

jokes.text: Are you sweating? / What happened to your face?
jokes.setup: Abbi and Ilana reunite on the street.
jokes.punchline: Each asks the other about their obvious physical state — Ilana is post-orgasm sweating, Abbi has partially Botoxed face — creating a mutual comedy of recognition.
jokes.explanation: Simultaneous reveals of their respective physical states — post-orgasm sweat and half-Botoxed face — is a strong visual comedy beat. The parallel structure of them both having something visibly wrong is a good payoff to the episode's two threads.

jokes.text: Then I felt like it's cooler to be, like, hot and young than warm, but then as the Botox was being injected into my face, I was like, it's...it's cooler to be warm.
jokes.setup: Abbi is explaining her emotional journey through the Botox session.
jokes.punchline: The 'hot/warm' contrast — hot (temperature/attractive) vs. warm (comfortable/authentic) — is resolved in favor of warmth literally and figuratively. Also, the space heater is still the actual goal.
jokes.explanation: The hot/warm wordplay connects the episode's literal (cold apartment, space heater) and metaphorical (aging, authenticity) themes. It's a thematic punchline that earns its place by having been set up from the first scene.

jokes.text: Botox? Abbi, what?
jokes.setup: Abbi mentions she had Botox.
jokes.punchline: Ilana — the least judgmental person alive — expresses genuine surprise, which implies Abbi has done something unusual enough to register on Ilana's scale.
jokes.explanation: Short but effective — Ilana's shock is the joke because we know her scale of what's shocking. Abbi getting Botox registers more than Ilana naming her vulva 'Abbi.'

jokes.text: Oh, my God, my table's gone!
jokes.setup: Abbi and Ilana have been talking when Abbi notices her table.
jokes.punchline: The card table Abbi was selling from has disappeared while she was in the Botox appointment — all her remaining cards gone.
jokes.explanation: Plot beat that sets up the note from Margo. Not particularly funny in itself but leads to the witch invitation, so it earns its place structurally.

jokes.text: 'Dear Young Abbi, at the start of the winter solstice tonight, come into the thicket of the park. You'll know when you've arrived.' / Oh, God, that is witchy!
jokes.setup: Abbi's table is gone and a note is left in its place.
jokes.punchline: The note from Margo is beautifully portentous — and Ilana's response 'Oh, God, that is witchy!' delivers it with maximum satisfaction.
jokes.explanation: 'You'll know when you've arrived' as a witch cliché, and Ilana's 'That is witchy!' approval — she's been waiting for this. The payoff of the Margo setup works well.
